chain_of_thought: Top non-allocator frame is the JNI function Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode; crash occurs in strlen_default, which is a libc helper, likely due to passing it a non-null-terminated or invalid pointer.
Ghidra MCP could not locate the specific function names (404 / not found), so I cannot inspect the implementation, memcpy usage, or argument derivations directly.

Given only the stack trace, the most plausible pattern is that the JNI Base64 decode implementation calls strlen on attacker-controlled input (the fuzzer-provided buffer mapped to a jbyteArray/jstring) without ensuring null-termination or correct bounds.

Calling strlen on arbitrary bytes can cause out-of-bounds reads until a 0 byte is found. This is a classic bug pattern if the code expects a C-string but receives length-delimited data or raw bytes from Java/fuzzer.

Because this is a Base64 decode JNI method, it is realistically reachable by app code taking untrusted input, and there is no indication the fuzzer did something impossible for production (like violating JNI type contracts): passing arbitrary content inside a valid Java String/byte[] is realistic.
The crash type (strlen_default) implies an out-of-bounds read, which is a memory safety issue, even if exploitation beyond DoS is unclear; it still qualifies as a vulnerability.
Without symbol access I can’t trace the full dataflow or confirm additional unsafe operations, so I keep confidence below 0.9 but above 0.6 based on typical implementations.
reasons: Crash occurs inside strlen_default called from Base64 JNI decode function, indicating strlen is used on untrusted input
Base64 decode APIs often incorrectly treat length-delimited Java data as C-strings, causing out-of-bounds reads when no NUL is present
The path is realistic (public Base64 utility), so attacker-controlled input can likely reach this code under normal app use
recommendations: Manually inspect Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode in libDSToolkitV30Jni.so to confirm how it computes input length and calls strlen
Replace strlen with a length-bounded variant (e.g., strnlen or explicit length from Java) and ensure only valid, null-terminated buffers are passed to C-string APIs
Add bounds checks or defensive validation for Base64 inputs coming from Java before decoding
assumptions: JNI Base64 decode function is reachable from regular app code and exposed to untrusted input
The fuzzer’s use of the JNI method matches realistic Java usage (valid types but arbitrary contents)
strlen_default crash is due to reading past the end of a non-null-terminated buffer from attacker-influenced data
limitations: Ghidra MCP could not load or decompile the target functions, so analysis is based solely on stack trace and common patterns
Exact argument values and validation logic around strlen are unknown
I cannot see whether other defenses (e.g., ASLR, hardened allocators) limit exploitability beyond DoS
